title: General relativity
text: General relativity, also known as the general theory of relativity and Einstein's theory of gravity, is the geometric theory of gravitation published by Albert Einstein in 1916. It generalizes special relativity and refines Newton's law of universal gravitation, providing a unified description of gravity as a geometric property of space and time, or four-dimensional spacetime. The curvature of spacetime is directly related to the energy, momentum, and stress of whatever matter and radiation are present.

title: Introduction to general relativity
text: General relativity is a theory of gravitation developed by Albert Einstein between 1907 and 1915. The theory says that the observed gravitational effect between masses results from their warping of spacetime. Newton's law of universal gravitation had been accepted for over two hundred years as a valid description of the gravitational force between masses, but general relativity superseded it by showing gravity emerges from the curvature of spacetime itself.

title: Electron
text: The electron is a subatomic particle with a negative one elementary electric charge. Electrons belong to the first generation of the lepton particle family and are generally thought to be elementary particles because they have no known components or substructure. The electron has a mass that is approximately 1/1836 that of the proton. Quantum mechanical properties of the electron include an intrinsic angular momentum (spin) of one-half, and quantum tunneling through barriers.

title: Hydrogen
text: Hydrogen is a chemical element with the symbol H and atomic number 1. It is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe, constituting roughly 75% of all normal matter by mass. Under standard conditions, hydrogen is a gas of diatomic molecules with the formula H₂. It is colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-toxic, and highly combustible. Hydrogen was first formally recognized and described as a distinct substance by Henry Cavendish in 1766.

title: Quark
text: A quark is a type of elementary particle and a fundamental constituent of matter. Quarks combine to form composite particles called hadrons, the most stable of which are protons and neutrons, the components of atomic nuclei. All commonly observable matter is composed of up quarks, down quarks, and electrons. Owing to color confinement, quarks are never found in isolation; they can be found only within hadrons, or in quark–gluon plasmas. Quarks have various intrinsic properties including electric charge, mass, color charge, and spin.

title: Speed of light
text: The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted c, is a universal physical constant equal to 299,792,458 meters per second. It is exact because the meter is defined in terms of c. Light travels faster in a vacuum than through any transparent medium. According to special relativity, c is the upper limit for the speed at which conventional matter, energy, or information can travel through space. All forms of electromagnetic radiation, including visible light, travel at the speed c in a vacuum.

title: Star
text: A star is an astronomical object comprising a luminous spheroid of plasma held together by self-gravity. The nearest star to Earth is the Sun. Many other stars are visible to the naked eye at night, appearing as a multitude of fixed luminous points. Stars are formed within molecular clouds, collapsing regions of interstellar gas. Stellar nucleosynthesis is the set of nuclear fusion reactions that produce the energy that makes stars shine, and it creates most of the elements heavier than hydrogen and helium.

title: Sun
text: The Sun is the star at the center of the Solar System. It is a massive, nearly perfect sphere of hot plasma, heated to incandescence by nuclear fusion reactions in its core. The Sun radiates this energy mainly as light, ultraviolet, and infrared radiation, and is the most important source of energy for life on Earth. Its diameter is about 1.39 million km, roughly 109 times that of Earth, and its mass is about 330,000 times that of Earth, accounting for about 99.86% of the total mass of the Solar System.

title: M-theory
text: M-theory is a theory in physics that unifies all consistent versions of superstring theory. Edward Witten first conjectured the existence of such a theory at a string theory conference in 1995. Witten's announcement initiated a flurry of research activity known as the second superstring revolution. The theory attempts to reconcile string theory with 11-dimensional supergravity. M-theory is not fully defined, but it suggests that the fundamental constituents of the universe are not point particles but rather vibrating strings or membranes.

title: Photoelectric effect
text: The photoelectric effect is the emission of electrons when electromagnetic radiation, such as light, hits a material. Electrons emitted in this manner are called photoelectrons. The phenomenon was first observed in 1887 by Heinrich Hertz. Albert Einstein explained the photoelectric effect in 1905 by proposing that light travels in discrete quanta called photons. The photoelectric effect demonstrated the particle nature of light and was a key piece of evidence for the development of quantum mechanics.

title: Black hole
text: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, including light or other electromagnetic waves, has enough speed to escape it. The boundary of no escape is called the event horizon. Although the event horizon has a large effect on the fate and circumstances of an object crossing it, it has no locally detectable features according to general relativity. In many ways, a black hole acts like an ideal black body, as it reflects no light.

title: Nuclear fission
text: Nuclear fission is a reaction in which the nucleus of an atom splits into two or more smaller nuclei. The fission process often produces gamma photons, and releases a very large amount of energy even by the energetic standards of radioactive decay. Nuclear fission was discovered by Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann in 1938, and the theoretical explanation was provided by Lise Meitner and Otto Frisch. It is the reaction exploited in nuclear reactors and nuclear weapons.

title: Quantum entanglement
text: Quantum entanglement is the phenomenon where the quantum state of each particle in a group of particles cannot be described independently of the state of the others, even when the particles are separated by a large distance. Measurements of physical properties such as position, momentum, spin, and polarization performed on entangled particles can be correlated. Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen used entanglement to argue that quantum mechanics was incomplete, while Bell's theorem later showed that the correlations cannot be explained by classical local hidden variables.

title: Higgs boson
text: The Higgs boson is an elementary particle in the Standard Model of particle physics produced by the quantum excitation of the Higgs field, one of the fields in particle physics theory. In the Standard Model, the Higgs particle is a massive scalar boson with zero spin, even (positive) parity, no electric charge, and no colour charge that couples to mass. It is the only known scalar particle that exists in nature. It was predicted theoretically in 1964 by Peter Higgs and others, and confirmed experimentally in 2012 at CERN.

title: Chicago Pile-1
text: Chicago Pile-1 (CP-1) was the world's first artificial nuclear reactor. CP-1 was built on a racquetball court under the stands of Stagg Field at the University of Chicago. The first artificial self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction was initiated in CP-1 on December 2, 1942. The construction and operation of CP-1 was a crucial step in the Manhattan Project's effort to build an atomic bomb during World War II.

title: Manhattan Project
text: The Manhattan Project was a research and development undertaking during World War II that produced the first nuclear weapons. It was led by the United States with the support of the United Kingdom and Canada. From 1942 to 1946, the project was under the direction of Major General Leslie Groves of the US Army Corps of Engineers. The project's scientific director was J. Robert Oppenheimer. The two types of atomic bombs developed—a uranium gun-type bomb and a plutonium implosion bomb—were detonated over the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945.

title: Trinity (nuclear test)
text: Trinity was the code name of the first detonation of a nuclear weapon, conducted by the United States Army at 5:29 a.m. on July 16, 1945, as part of the Manhattan Project. The test was conducted in the Jornada del Muerto desert of New Mexico, on the White Sands Proving Ground. The device tested was an implosion-design plutonium bomb, nicknamed 'The Gadget'. The explosion released energy equivalent to roughly 25 kilotons of TNT.

title: Superconductivity
text: Superconductivity is a set of physical properties observed in certain materials where electrical resistance vanishes and magnetic flux fields are expelled from the material when cooled below a characteristic critical temperature. The electrical resistance of a superconducting material drops abruptly to zero when the material is cooled below its critical temperature. Superconductivity was discovered by Dutch physicist Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911. The Meissner effect, the expulsion of any magnetic field from the interior of a superconductor, is one of its defining properties.

title: Double-slit experiment
text: In modern physics, the double-slit experiment is a demonstration that light and matter can display characteristics of both classically defined waves and particles. This experiment has now been performed for photons, electrons, atoms, and even molecules. Thomas Young performed the original double-slit experiment with light in 1801, demonstrating the wave nature of light. When quantum particles are sent through the slits one at a time, an interference pattern still builds up, suggesting each particle somehow passes through both slits simultaneously.

title: Hubble Space Telescope
text: The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is a space telescope that was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990 and remains in operation. It was not the first space telescope, but it is one of the largest and most versatile. Named after astronomer Edwin Hubble, the Hubble telescope has provided some of the most detailed visible-light images of objects billions of light-years away and has enabled scientists to measure the expansion rate of the universe with unprecedented precision.

title: Bacteria
text: Bacteria are ubiquitous, mostly free-living organisms often consisting of one biological cell. They constitute a large domain of prokaryotic microorganisms. Bacteria were among the first life forms to appear on Earth, and are present in most of its habitats. Bacteria inhabit soil, water, acidic hot springs, radioactive waste, and the deep biosphere of Earth's crust. Bacteria play a vital role in many stages of the nutrient cycle by recycling nutrients such as the fixation of nitrogen from the atmosphere.

title: Virus
text: A virus is a submicroscopic infectious agent that replicates only inside the living cells of an organism. Viruses infect all life forms, from animals and plants to microorganisms, including bacteria and archaea. Viruses are found in almost every ecosystem on Earth and are the most numerous type of biological entity. Since Dmitri Ivanovsky's 1892 article describing a non-bacterial pathogen infecting tobacco plants, and the discovery of the tobacco mosaic virus by Martinus Beijerinck in 1898, about 11,000 of the millions of virus species have been described in detail.

title: DNA
text: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is a polymer composed of two polynucleotide chains that coil around each other to form a double helix. It is a type of nucleic acid and carries genetic instructions for the development, functioning, growth, and reproduction of all known organisms and many viruses. DNA and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are nucleic acids. Alongside proteins, lipids, and complex carbohydrates (polysaccharides), nucleic acids are one of the four major types of macromolecules that are essential for all known forms of life.

title: Evolution
text: Evolution is the change in the heritable characteristics of biological populations over successive generations. It occurs when evolutionary processes such as natural selection and genetic drift act on genetic variation, resulting in certain characteristics becoming more or less common within a population over time. The process of evolution has resulted in the vast diversity of life on Earth. The theory of evolution by natural selection was independently proposed by both Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace and set out in detail in Darwin's 1859 book On the Origin of Species.

title: Genetics
text: Genetics is the study of genes, genetic variation, and heredity in organisms. It is an important branch of biology because heredity is vital to the evolution of organisms. Gregor Mendel, a Moravian Augustinian friar working in the 19th century, was the first to study genetics scientifically. Mendel studied trait inheritance, patterns in the way traits are handed down from parents to offspring. He observed that organisms inherit traits via discrete units of inheritance, now called genes.

title: Immune system
text: The immune system is a network of biological processes that protects an organism from diseases. It detects and responds to a wide variety of pathogens, from viruses to parasitic worms, as well as cancer cells and objects such as wood splinters, distinguishing them from the organism's own healthy tissue. Many species have two major subsystems of the immune system: the innate immune system and the adaptive immune system. Both use molecules and cells to perform their functions.

title: Metabolism
text: Metabolism is the set of life-sustaining chemical reactions in organisms. The three main functions of metabolism are: the conversion of the energy in food to energy available to run cellular processes; the conversion of food to building blocks for proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, and some carbohydrates; and the elimination of metabolic wastes. These enzyme-catalyzed reactions allow organisms to grow and reproduce, maintain their structures, and respond to their environments.

title: Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event
text: The Cretaceous–Paleogene (K–Pg) extinction event, also known as the Cretaceous–Tertiary (K–T) extinction, was a sudden mass extinction of three-quarters of the plant and animal species on Earth, approximately 66 million years ago. The event caused the extinction of all non-avian dinosaurs. Most paleontologists today believe the event was caused by a massive asteroid impact near what is now Chicxulub, Mexico, combined with volcanic activity at the Deccan Traps.

title: History of biology
text: The history of biology traces the study of the living world from ancient to modern times. Although the concept of biology as a single coherent field arose in the 19th century, the biological sciences emerged from traditions of medicine and natural history reaching back to ancient Egypt, with contributions from ancient Greece, the Islamic Golden Age, and the European Renaissance. Key figures include Aristotle, William Harvey, Carl Linnaeus, Gregor Mendel, Charles Darwin, and Francis Crick and James Watson.

title: History of evolutionary thought
text: The history of evolutionary thought explores how ideas about organic evolution developed from ancient times to the modern evolutionary synthesis. Ancient Greek philosophers, including Anaximander and Empedocles, speculated about the natural origin of species. After a long period of stasis during the Middle Ages, the Scientific Revolution led to new perspectives. Jean-Baptiste Lamarck proposed the first formal theory of evolution in the early 19th century, later superseded by Darwin's theory of natural selection.

title: Introduction to viruses
text: Viruses are the most numerous biological entities on Earth and are found in almost every ecosystem. A virus is a tiny infectious agent that can only replicate inside the cells of a living host. Viruses infect all types of life forms, from animals and plants to microorganisms such as bacteria. Viruses are not considered living organisms because they lack the ability to independently carry out metabolic processes. Since the first virus was discovered in 1899, around 6,000 virus species have been described.

title: RNA interference
text: RNA interference (RNAi) is a biological process in which RNA molecules are involved in sequence-specific suppression of gene expression by double-stranded RNA through translational or transcriptional repression. Historically, RNAi was known by other names including co-suppression, post-transcriptional gene silencing (PTGS), and quelling. The process was first described in plants, and the key discovery that small RNA molecules guide gene silencing was made by Andrew Fire and Craig Mello, who shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2006.

title: Phagocyte
text: Phagocytes are cells that protect the body by ingesting harmful foreign particles, bacteria, and dead or dying cells. Their name comes from the Greek phagein, meaning to eat, and kytos, meaning hollow vessel. They are essential for fighting infections and for subsequent immunity. Phagocytes are crucial in the innate immune system and are found in the blood, tissues, and lymph nodes. Key types include neutrophils, monocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells.

title: Flower
text: A flower, sometimes known as a bloom or blossom, is the reproductive structure found in flowering plants (plants of the division Angiospermae). The biological function of a flower is to facilitate reproduction, usually by providing a mechanism for the union of sperm with eggs. Flowers may facilitate outcrossing or allow selfing. Some flowers produce diaspores without fertilization. Flowers contain sporangia and are the site where gametophytes develop. Flowers give rise to fruit and seeds.

title: On the Origin of Species
text: On the Origin of Species (or, more completely, On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life) is an 1859 work of scientific literature by Charles Darwin which is considered to be the foundation of evolutionary biology. Darwin's book introduced the scientific theory that populations evolve over the course of generations through a process of natural selection. It presented evidence from different fields of biology supporting the idea of common descent.

title: Ediacaran biota
text: The Ediacaran biota is a collection of macroscopic organisms from the Ediacaran period (approximately 635–538.8 million years ago). These organisms are considered the oldest known complex multicellular organisms. The organisms preserved as fossils in Ediacaran-aged rock are mostly soft-bodied, of uncertain phylogenetic affinity. Many Ediacaran organisms are unique and not related to later groups. They are named after the Ediacara Hills of South Australia, where they were first described.

title: Archaea
text: Archaea constitute a domain of single-celled organisms. These microorganisms lack cell nuclei and are therefore prokaryotes. Archaea were initially classified as bacteria, receiving the name archaebacteria, but this classification is outdated. The Archaea are now recognized as a domain in the three-domain system. Archaea are often found in extreme environments, including hydrothermal vents, hot springs, salt lakes, and the deep ocean. They are abundant in soil, oceans, and as part of the human microbiome.

title: Fertilisation of Orchids
text: On the Various Contrivances by which British and Foreign Orchids are Fertilised by Insects is an 1862 book by Charles Darwin. It was his first book after On the Origin of Species, and was the first to detail how the intricate structures of orchid flowers had evolved specifically to promote cross-pollination by insects. Darwin described the mechanisms of different orchid genera and drew on his concept of natural selection to explain them, demonstrating evolution in action through a detailed case study.

title: Cell (biology)
text: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all forms of life. Every cell consists of cytoplasm enclosed within a membrane; many cells also contain organelles. There are two broad categories of cells: eukaryotic cells, which contain a nucleus, and prokaryotic cells, which do not. Prokaryotes are single-celled organisms such as bacteria, while eukaryotes can be either single-celled or multicellular. In multicellular organisms, every cell in the organism's body derives ultimately from a single cell in a fertilized egg.

title: Natural selection
text: Natural selection is the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in phenotype. It is a key mechanism of evolution, the change in the heritable traits characteristic of a population over generations. Charles Darwin popularised the term natural selection, contrasting it with artificial selection, which in his view is intentional, whereas natural selection is not. Variation exists within all populations of organisms. This occurs partly because random mutations arise in the genome of an individual organism, and their offspring can inherit such mutations.

title: Æthelstan
text: Æthelstan (also spelled Athelstan) was King of the Anglo-Saxons from 924 and King of the English from 927 to 939. He was the first King of England. He is considered one of the greatest Anglo-Saxon kings. His victory at the Battle of Brunanburh in 937 was celebrated in verse in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle. He died childless in 939 and was succeeded by his younger half-brother Edmund.

title: Æthelflæd
text: Æthelflæd (also Aethelflaed) was the Lady of the Mercians from 911 until her death in 918. The eldest daughter of King Alfred the Great, she married Æthelred, Lord of the Mercians in the 880s. After her husband died in 911, she ruled Mercia alone. She played a key role in defending England against the Danes and Norsemen, building a series of burhs (fortified towns) across Mercia and leading military campaigns against the Vikings in the East Midlands and the north of England.

title: Æthelred the Unready
text: Æthelred II (966–1016), known as 'the Unready,' was King of England from 978 to 1013 and again from 1014 to 1016. 'Unready' is a mistranslation of Old English unræd, meaning 'poorly advised.' His reign was marked by conflict with the Danes; he ordered the Saint Brice's Day massacre of Danes in England in 1002. He was briefly ousted by Danish king Sweyn Forkbeard in 1013 and died in exile before returning to claim the throne, dying before seeing off the Danish threat permanently.

title: Manhattan Project
text: The Manhattan Project was a research and development undertaking during World War II that produced the first nuclear weapons. Led by the United States with support from the UK and Canada, it ran from 1942 to 1946. The scientific director was J. Robert Oppenheimer. The project culminated in the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, killing an estimated 129,000 to 226,000 people and contributing to Japan's surrender and the end of World War II.

title: 1975 Australian constitutional crisis
text: The 1975 Australian constitutional crisis, also known as 'The Dismissal,' was a political crisis in Australia in which the Governor-General, Sir John Kerr, dismissed the government of Prime Minister Gough Whitlam. It was the first and only time in Australian history that a prime minister has been removed from office by a governor-general. The crisis was triggered by the Senate's refusal to pass supply bills, causing a constitutional standoff. Malcolm Fraser was then appointed caretaker prime minister and won the subsequent general election.

title: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
text: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan was the fifth Umayyad caliph, reigning from 685 until his death in 705. He is considered one of the most important caliphs of the Umayyad dynasty. He consolidated Umayyad rule after a period of civil war, implemented major administrative reforms, and Arabized the government bureaucracy. He oversaw the construction of the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem in 692, one of the oldest extant Islamic monuments, and introduced a new gold coinage standardized across the caliphate.

title: Abbasid invasion of Asia Minor (806)
text: The Abbasid invasion of Asia Minor in 806 was a major military campaign led by Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid against the Byzantine Empire. It was the largest Arab incursion into Byzantine territory to that point and resulted in the humiliating surrender of Empress Irene's successor, Nikephoros I, who was forced to pay tribute to the caliph. The campaign demonstrated the military power of the Abbasid caliphate at its height but had no lasting territorial consequences.

title: French Revolution
text: The French Revolution was a period of radical political and societal transformation in France that began with the Estates General of 1789 and ended with the formation of the French Consulate in November 1799. Many of its ideas are considered fundamental principles of liberal democracy, while the phrases 'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity' are still the national motto of France. The Revolution overthrew the monarchy, established a republic, culminated in dictatorship under Napoleon Bonaparte, and abolished the feudal system.

title: Byzantine Empire
text: The Byzantine Empire, also known as the Eastern Roman Empire, was the continuation of the Roman Empire in its eastern provinces during Late Antiquity and the Middle Ages, when its capital city was Constantinople. It survived the fragmentation and fall of the Western Roman Empire in the 5th century and continued to exist for an additional thousand years until the fall of Constantinople to the Ottoman Turks in 1453. The Byzantines preserved Greco-Roman culture and transmitted it to medieval Europe.

title: Black Death
text: The Black Death, also known as the Pestilence and the Plague, was one of the most devastating pandemics in human history, killing 30–60% of Europe's population between 1347 and 1351. It is attributed to the bacterium Yersinia pestis. Originating in Central Asia, it traveled along Silk Road trade routes and reached Crimea by 1346, then spread to Sicily and mainland Europe by 1347. The Black Death had lasting effects on European society, economy, and religion.

title: Mongol Empire
text: The Mongol Empire of the 13th and 14th centuries was the largest contiguous land empire in history. Founded by Genghis Khan in 1206, it eventually stretched from East Asia to central Europe, encompassing roughly 24 million square kilometers. At its peak, it controlled most of the Asian continent along with much of Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and parts of Southeast Asia. The empire facilitated trade and cultural exchange along the Silk Road but also caused devastating destruction and population decline through warfare.

title: Industrial Revolution
text: The Industrial Revolution was a period of global transition of human economy towards more widespread, efficient, and stable manufacturing processes that succeeded the Agricultural Revolution. Beginning in Great Britain around 1760, it spread worldwide and lasted through about 1840. This transition included going from hand production methods to machines; new chemical manufacturing and iron production processes; the increasing use of steam power and water power; and the development of machine tools.

title: Cold War
text: The Cold War was a period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union that began following World War II (1947) and lasted until the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991. It featured military coalitions, covert operations, proxy wars, nuclear arms race, the space race, and economic competition. It was called 'cold' because there was no large-scale fighting between the two superpowers directly. The conflict divided the world into blocs, the Western Bloc led by the US and the Eastern Bloc led by the USSR.

title: World War I
text: World War I or the First World War (28 July 1914 – 11 November 1918) was a global conflict fought in Europe and elsewhere. It was the deadliest and most widespread war in history up to that point, involving more than 30 countries and resulting in approximately 20 million deaths. The war was caused by a complex set of factors including militarism, alliances, imperialism, and nationalism. The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand triggered the crisis; the Treaty of Versailles (1919) formally ended the war.

title: World War II
text: World War II or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945) was a global conflict that involved virtually every part of the world. The main combatants were the Allied powers (led by Britain, the US, and the Soviet Union) against the Axis powers (Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, and Imperial Japan). It was the deadliest conflict in history, causing 70–85 million fatalities, including deliberate genocide and the Holocaust. It ended with the unconditional surrender of Germany and Japan in 1945.

title: Age of Enlightenment
text: The Age of Enlightenment (also known as the Age of Reason or simply the Enlightenment) was an intellectual and philosophical movement that dominated Europe in the 17th and 18th centuries. The Enlightenment included a range of ideas centered on the value of human happiness, the pursuit of knowledge obtained by means of reason and the evidence of the senses, and ideals such as natural law, liberty, progress, toleration, fraternity, constitutional government, and separation of church and state.

title: American Revolution
text: The American Revolution was an ideological and political revolution that occurred in British America between 1765 and 1791. The Americans in the Thirteen Colonies formed independent states that defeated the British Empire in the American Revolutionary War, gaining independence from the British Crown and establishing the United States of America. The American political tradition was built on Enlightenment ideas about natural rights, consent of the governed, and separation of powers.

title: Smyth Report
text: The Smyth Report (formally titled Atomic Energy for Military Purposes) is the common name of an official account of the Manhattan Project, the Allied effort to develop nuclear weapons during World War II. Written by physicist Henry DeWolf Smyth, it was released to the public on August 12, 1945, days after the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. It was intended to inform the public about the nature of the work done, while keeping technical details classified.

title: MAUD Committee
text: The MAUD Committee was a British scientific committee that met during World War II to study the feasibility of developing an atomic bomb. Formed in April 1940, it produced two reports in July 1941 concluding that an atomic bomb was feasible and could be produced before the end of the war. The committee's conclusions were decisive in convincing both the British government and the United States to pursue nuclear weapon development, directly leading to the Manhattan Project.

title: Crusades
text: The Crusades were a series of religious wars initiated, supported, and sometimes directed by the Latin Church in the medieval period. The best known of these Crusades are those to the Holy Land in the period between 1095 and 1291 that were intended to recover Jerusalem and the surrounding area from Islamic rule. Beginning with the First Crusade called by Pope Urban II in 1095, they were marked by significant cultural exchange and left a lasting imprint on the politics of the Middle East.

The infinite series 1 − 2 + 3 − 4 + ⋯ is a divergent series in mathematics. Its partial sums oscillate between 0 and 1, but the sequence of partial sums does not converge. However, the series can be assigned a value of 1/4 using various summation methods such as Cesàro summation and Abel summation. Leonhard Euler performed early work on this series, and it appears in many areas of modern physics and string theory.

title: Algebra
text: Algebra is the branch of mathematics dealing with symbols and the rules for manipulating those symbols; it is a unifying thread of almost all of mathematics. Elementary algebra deals with the manipulation of variables as if they were numbers. Modern algebra is more broadly the study of mathematical symbols and the rules for manipulating those symbols, encompassing abstract algebra, linear algebra, and related fields. The word algebra comes from the Arabic word al-jabr, from the title of the 9th-century treatise by Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi.

title: Euclidean algorithm
text: In mathematics, the Euclidean algorithm, or Euclid's algorithm, is an efficient method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers (numbers), the largest number that divides them both without a remainder. It is named after the ancient Greek mathematician Euclid, who first described it in his Elements (c. 300 BC). It is an example of an algorithm, a step-by-step procedure for performing a calculation according to well-defined rules, and is one of the oldest algorithms in common use.

title: Logarithm
text: In mathematics, the logarithm is the inverse function to exponentiation. That means the logarithm of a number x to the base b is the exponent to which b must be raised to produce x. For example, since 1000 = 10³, the logarithm base 10 of 1000 is 3, or log₁₀(1000) = 3. The logarithm base 10 is called the common logarithm and is used in science and engineering. The natural logarithm has the irrational base e ≈ 2.718, and is broadly used in mathematics and physics.

title: Pi
text: The number π (/paɪ/; spelled out as 'pi') is a mathematical constant that is the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter, approximately equal to 3.14159. The number π appears in many formulae across mathematics and physics. It is an irrational number, meaning that it cannot be expressed exactly as a ratio of two integers, although fractions such as 22/7 are commonly used to approximate it. Consequently, its decimal representation never ends, nor enters a permanently repeating pattern.

title: Logic
text: Logic is the study of correct reasoning. It includes both formal and informal logic. Formal logic is the study of deductively valid inferences or logical truths. It examines how conclusions follow from premises in a topic-neutral way and without any content-specific assumptions. Mathematical logic is a branch of formal logic concerned with proofs, computability, model theory, and set theory. Logic is used in most intellectual activities, especially in philosophy, mathematics, and computer science.

title: Group (mathematics)
text: In mathematics, a group is a set with an operation that satisfies four conditions: closure, associativity, identity, and invertibility. Many mathematical structures are groups endowed with other properties. For example, the integers with the addition operation form a group. The concept of a group arose from the study of polynomial equations, starting with Évariste Galois in the 1830s, and abstract algebra built on the concept thereafter. Groups are central in abstract algebra, geometry, number theory, and many branches of physics.

title: Pi (π)
text: Pi is a transcendental number, meaning it is not the root of any non-zero polynomial with rational coefficients. The transcendence of π was proved by Ferdinand von Lindemann in 1882. Its first few digits are 3.14159265358979. Archimedes was among the first to estimate π rigorously, computing it to be between 223/71 and 22/7. In the modern era, computations have extended π to trillions of digits. It appears in many contexts beyond circles, including probability theory, statistics, and complex analysis.

title: Fermat's Last Theorem
text: Fermat's Last Theorem states that no three positive integers a, b, and c can satisfy the equation aⁿ + bⁿ = cⁿ for any integer value of n greater than 2. The theorem was first conjectured by Pierre de Fermat in 1637 in the margin of a copy of Arithmetica, where he claimed to have a proof that was too large to fit in the margin. The first successful proof was released in 1994 by Andrew Wiles, after 358 years; it was completed with help from Richard Taylor.

title: Problem of Apollonius
text: In Euclidean plane geometry, Apollonius's problem is to construct circles that are tangent to three given circles in a plane. Apollonius of Perga (c. 262 – c. 190 BC) posed and solved this famous problem in his work Ἐπαφαί (Tangencies); this work has been lost, but a 4th-century report of its contents survives. Solving this problem has served as a touchstone for various geometric methods, including those of René Descartes, Isaac Newton, and inversive geometry.

title: Parity of zero
text: Zero is an even number. In other words, its parity—the quality of an integer being even or odd—is even. This can be easily verified based on the definition of 'even': it is an integer multiple of 2, specifically 0 × 2. As a result, zero shares all the properties that characterize even numbers: for example, 0 is neighbored on both sides by odd numbers, any decimal integer has the same parity as its last digit, and so on. Zero also fits into the patterns formed by other even numbers.

title: Affine symmetric group
text: The affine symmetric group is a mathematical structure that describes the symmetries of the number line in terms of reflections and translations, and of the regular triangular tessellation of the plane. As with the ordinary symmetric group, the elements of the affine symmetric group may be represented as permutations of the integers. The affine symmetric group has several important uses in combinatorics and representation theory, and connections to number theory, Lie theory, and the theory of symmetric functions.

title: Laplace–Runge–Lenz vector
text: In classical mechanics, the Laplace–Runge–Lenz (LRL) vector is a vector used chiefly to describe the shape and orientation of the orbit of one astronomical body around another, such as a planet revolving around a star. For two bodies interacting by Newtonian gravity, the LRL vector is a constant of motion, meaning that it is the same no matter where it is calculated on the orbit. It has been used in understanding the hydrogen atom in quantum mechanics and the SO(4) symmetry of the Kepler problem.

title: Mirror symmetry (string theory)
text: In algebraic geometry and theoretical physics, mirror symmetry is a relationship between geometric objects called Calabi–Yau manifolds. The term refers to a situation where two Calabi–Yau manifolds look very different geometrically but are nevertheless equivalent when used as extra dimensions of string theory. Mirror symmetry was first observed in physics by Dixon, Lerche, Vafa, and Warner in 1989. Mathematically, it predicts surprising relationships between the numbers of curves on different manifolds.

title: Quine–Putnam indispensability argument
text: The Quine–Putnam indispensability argument is an argument in the philosophy of mathematics for the existence of abstract mathematical objects such as numbers and sets, based on the premise that mathematics is indispensable to our best scientific theories. The argument originates in the work of Willard Van Orman Quine and Hilary Putnam. It proceeds: we should believe in entities that are indispensable to our best scientific theories; mathematics is indispensable to our best theories; therefore, we should believe in mathematical entities.
